speaker: interviewer
text: This is an interview with Ellis Lewin, and it is tape number three. Mr. Lewin, you described your apartment to us at the end of the last tape, and I was wondering how many people lived in-- with you, again, before the war broke out? And did the number of people change first in your suite or in your building during the time of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the-- the number of people remained the same because we were not dislodged since we lived there before the war-- the war, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we-- we basically lived-- there was the same amount of people till we left the ghetto as we did before. So there was my family, my sister, and myself, my dad, and an aunt-- a sister of my mother's who was with us prior to the warr-- also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what a day would have been like for you, a typical day during the time of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The typical day is something that-- you get up in the morning, you [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you daven still?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't daven. We-- we didn't daven. And go to work, and you're hungry. You know, when you're hungry, that's-- your typical day begins when you're hungry and your typical day ends when you're hungry. When you're not hungry, you think of things to do and-- other than that-- other than just that. And so, since I was always hungry, my day always began with being hungry and couldn't wait until I eat the next time and the next time. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You were observing things that you'd sort of become almost oblivious to, that would shock you some other time. You see now people laying on the street from starvation, from hunger. You see death of someone is becoming a very common and normal [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] acceptance. Death, in fact, just becomes a matter of fact thing on a daily basis.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sickness is prevalent, like common knowledge. It's everywhere. And you hear rumors and you hear conversation of nothing but the end-- the end of everything. And what do we need to live? And what is there left?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they began to then take the-- the-- the younger people were now being taken out of the ghetto. They were asked to come and work. And they would tell them that they would just leave for a week or two, because they needed them to build certain roads and so on, and that they would a-- reward them. And you see more young people being ta-- leaving the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the ghetto. During the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any idea about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943, '43, '44, beginning of '44. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There's sta-- there has now become a little unrest, you know. There's just-- You are now living un-- under terrible, terrible conditions. Since the Germans themselves are now, as I see, and I'm able now to see why these things happen, not getting enough food for their--

speaker: interviewer
text: So they are now denying the little ra-- the rations that we used to get were now cut down to virtually a minimum ration. And so everything that we had when the ghetto started and as it was progressing for a while was now being taken away. And now you're starting to see the-- the-- the results of hunger and sickness and so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And while there was hope that we're going to be liberated somehow by the Russians-- there was no such thing as mentioning about Americans, since Russia was the next place to us-- it is now becoming a-- a-- a-- rumor that we're going some other places. And now the-- again, the conversation in family and in circles and wherever you go as a young boy. Whether you go to work, you hear the same thing. There's no more playing out in the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: There is nothing to play with. It's-- you're hungry. And when you get home, you hear the same thing, you know. What's going to happen to us? How are we going to go now? Everyone is talking very openly, whereas before, it was a little more subdued.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now everyone is talking openly that maybe this is the end and so on. So this is the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] beginning of the end of the ghetto, as far as I as a child was able to compare, from the beginning of the ghetto as to my existence. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get your rations?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were rationed. We were rationed. We'd go and get vegetables. There was x amount of vegetables, there was-- They would give the-- the bakers a certain amount of dough and things, and you had to go there with tickets. And there were soup lines which you had to give tickets to. And it was organized. It was organized. And it was controlled by-- by the-- within the-- within the government, within the Jewish government itself. There's a lot of improprieties, as-- as could be expected. Some ate more, and some others got less. And some were treated better, and others weren't. Some were able to still procure some medication through some black market [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ways. But where there is a way, there was a mean. There's always a way for someone else to benefit more than others.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were those rumors or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no there were--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that firsthand?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw that. Yes, absolutely. I had an uncle who was in charge of my mother's sis-- brother, who was in charge of the-- what they call the prison camp, and who lived quite well and ate quite well, and was able to do the things that-- get the things that none of us could ever dream of. And there were many people like that who were privy and who were special people in that government, and that-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the system. And how and why. Today I see why, but then I didn't. So they were able to impress and do things to impress-- to reach or acquire those kind of positions.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghetto?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I did not.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, how long did you work each day?

speaker: interviewee
text: About five hours, six hours. I think. I think. I don't really recall, you know. But I worked every day except Saturdays. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was kind of like a fun thing, you know. You were with other kids, and we sort of-- and [INAUDIBLE] the elders would show you how to do this. And it was a thing to do, really, rather than no-- nothing to do. Because like I say, it's-- it was just not a nice way of growing up. There was nothing to look forward to. So sometimes, moving from one place to another was a-- a big thrill, a big happening, or something. Better than sitting around in the house and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it mainly other children who were working with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was oth-- no, adults. There was adults and children and women. It was a-- was a-- it was a place you had to go. You were delegated to go. It was-- you didn't apply for a job. It's just, that's what you got. They had metals. They were-- they had metal shops. They had leather shops. They had lingerie-- lingerie shops. All these were being made for the Germans. And I recall when there would be a-- like an inspection, a visit. And it's just like you would see in some of those [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] movies or stories, that these people, you know, when they gave-- when they said they were coming to visit these factories. And these were all high-ranking officers with their wives, and it was an entourage of-- of-- of what I would call like the royalty. And we would all have to stand. And they came in and examined the way we were working and how we were doing these things. And the women were there. It was like a grand showing. It was like a-- a wonderful, wonderful happening for them. And they were-- they were examining the goods that were being made by us and shipped to them. So these inspections, I remember, would-- would be maybe two, three, or four times a year. They would come and examine how we worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your parents? Were they assigned jobs as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: My dad-- no, my mother was not. She-- she was-- she was sick. My mother was sick. She had a bad stomach, and so she couldn't do much. And my dad, of course, was a musician. And he was also a policeman in the-- in the ghetto. And his duties was to, I guess, guard this and go there. I'm not quite sure of exactly what his functions were. But he was part of the so-called government in the ghetto. And as a re-- thus as a result, we stayed longer and long-- We were the longest to stay there. Because as I-- as people realized or not, doctors and policemen and government people stayed on so that they could [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] finish the-- the-- the evacuation of the ghetto. And they needed all these people to do so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they told them that they'll be the last ones to go. Which was always, I guess, the best thing, is always to be the last to go, since where you were going, the rumors were that it wasn't a good place to go. So in that respect, it was a mitzvah. It was a good thing. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hidden. I was-- There was a time where there-- the-- we didn't-- we wanted to stay as long as we could. And our doctor, which my dad later married, hid me and my sister in a-- in a attic for I don't know how long. Maybe a few days, a week, or whatever it was. Because these things, when they were evacuating, they were taking them by blocks, by sections of the ghetto. So once a section was supposedly evacuated, they had the police come in there-- the Jewish police, the people that-- that-- that would run the internal government-- go from apartment to apartment to make sure that everybody was gone. And some of that search would require going through attics, basements, and so on. And, you know, people are very innovative and very-- so they would just find ways of not reporting. The system did not, I don't recall, and I'm not going to say that I knew, but I don't know that there was a roll call, you know, in terms of is a-- is Mr. Smith there, is Mr. Jones there. And it was just that you went by blocks, and then the search began. So if this street was to go, after the street supposedly is-- is gone and evacuated, then you send in the police to go through each place to make sure that everyone has-- has, in fact, left and gone with the evacuation.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when that block was gone, we were-- we were-- we were hidden up in her attic and-- We'll have to come back to that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a bit. Before we come to that, do you recall refugees coming into the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I recall there was always-- there were always some-- some outsiders coming in, Jews from other areas. I never had the-- the-- the privilege or the opportunity to meet them. But the conversation was yes, that there were people being coming in from little places. And it was like the-- like maybe Lodz was becoming the last place to get everyone together and get them out of there to-- to the next place, which would be the concentration camps. But yes, there were people that were being-- being really brought all the time from other places. Since the two major areas of ghettos that we still had was the Warsaw ghetto and the Lodz ghetto, they would use those facilities [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to-- to enable them to go to the next-- to the next step.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Back to the hiding. Just your sister and yourself? How about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: And my cousin. My cou-- My cousin, my-- my stepmother's nephew. We were hidden in the-- in the attic. And so when-- she was allowed to stay, because she was a doctor. She was a-- one of those people that was able-- was retained. And when they evacuated her section, the part of her street--

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your parents? Where were they at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- They were-- they were also in the place.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were in hiding, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. Absolutely, and because our section was already evacuated. And we were able to stay there for another few days. And finally, it was-- they hoarded her out, too. And with that, there was no one else left. She was-- she was-- she had to go to a place where the hospital facilities-- as a doctor, to a hospital facility, rather than to a house at that particular juncture. And then everybody, at that point, was supposedly evacuated to go out. And that was the last place that we were able to stay in the Lodz ghetto before we were-- before we were evacuated, which was 19-- I would say around August of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Be-- before we talk about the evacuation, is there anything else that you'd like to say about your experience in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, I-- I reiterate that the ghetto, to me, was still the last place that-- I wish I could have stayed in the ghetto under those conditions, even worse conditions. Any conditions, in fact, if I was able to stay there with my family and hold out until the liberation of-- of Lodz, and then make start our lives all over again, in whatever capacity, in whatever fashion or manner. And I say this to you because I say it as a child. And that's all I knew is to be as a child. And-- and again, I say to you as a child, the most important things in your world to you is your parents, your mother. And once that was gone, it just became a different ball game altogether. It just turned me and people like myself into a different human being. And it just was a-- was another phase, another way of existing. So the ghetto, to me, still represented the very good thing. Of all the bad things up to this point, it was a good thing because of being with my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your entire extended family make it into the ghetto until the very end, or was anybody deported earlier?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We were taken out in phases. Some were-- some-- some went a little earlier. But in actuality, that apartment where we lived, OK, was very close to her apartment where she lived. So it was a matter of maybe a day or so, or two days. We-- when we talk in those days, we don't talk weeks and months. It's hours and minutes and it's-- it's something that-- that you don't think long. Everything is from day to day. So we basically left at about the same time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ba-- Back to the hiding, then. Were you found, or did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we were not found. We were-- we heard the-- the-- the-- the noises of the-- of the search and conversation. And she was there, right there, and she was-- I'll tell you, she-- doctors in the ghetto were very privileged, because the Germans didn't have doctors. And believe it or not, they would go to the Jewish doctors if they-- if-- because of their fronts and their-- and their-- they were, at that point, starting to feel the wrath of the-- of the-- of their fronts and the-- and both in Russia and somewhere, so, and the American side. That all their doctors were being sent to all the fronts.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they would rely on the Jewish doctors. If they had something seriously wrong with them or whatever, they would firstly go to-- they would incor-- they would enlist a Jewish doctor to diagnose their-- their problem. It sounds a little bizarre, but it is a fact.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't care who says that. That's the way it was. And it's not by choice that they did it, but it was just another way of surviving for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as a doctor, she was a little bit more-- in terms of when they would go and search the house, they too had their own little interest in-- in mind. And said let-- well, I'm sure she's telling them the truth and so on. Since she was not married, so there would be no reason for her to have family or this-- or, you know, children and that and so on. So they just did a very quick search and went on. So we were able to be there another few days. In the scheme of things, it-- it didn't turn out as a permanent solution. But it was a few more days of being in the ghetto and hiding. But the ultimate result would have been, if we'd gone earlier, it would be the same place as we wound up at the-- at the end. Do you know why your parents chose to leave the place of hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were-- there was-- there was no one else there anymore. And you cannot get food. You cannot do anything. Once-- once-- once you evacuate an area, there's nothing that's supposed to move. Nothing. A dog, a cat, nothing. Because that's supposed to be a dead, total, complete, silent area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you can't sit there for weeks or months. There was no way of getting food anymore. There was no way of communicating with anything other than dying in the-- in the attic. So ultimately, you just succumbed to the fact that you're going to go to the next place and hoping that you can survive and-- and-- and move along.

speaker: interviewee
text: There, it was sure death, because there was nowhere else to receive anything in terms of food. So this is the reason we-- we continued on that journey and-- and going on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened, then, when you left the attic?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we were gathered, and-- and we-- we were basically already packed and everything. And we--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did that mean, being packed? What did you have? What were you taking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you were-- you were taking-- you were taking, like, bed-- bedding. You were taking, like, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] your worldly possessions. You became very creative, as-- as I will tell you in my next phase of this tale, so to speak. You took the things that you thought would-- would serve you best after his speech. Whether you believed it or not, you did go along with that, just in case.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we schlepped the pillow, this, and some clothes, some shoes. Shoes were very valuable. You don't go into a store and buy ready-made shoes. Every shoe you-- we wear and we wore was fitted and made by a schuster, by a shoemaker. So these were valuable things. And clothing. So in terms of what did we take, it was enough that you can carry, not [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] have someone carry for you or put it on some sort of a-- a transportation vehicle. And my dad took his-- he was a-- he was an excellent accordio-- accordion player, so he took his accordion.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother took-- she was a sew-- seamstress, too, and she knew, so she took some of that thing. And my sister was-- schlepped whatever she could, and I took whatever I could. So we just carried as much as we could to the train, to the-- to the place where we met to get on the trains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe that experience to me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 13 SECONDS] If there is such a thing as intuition-- like a seventh, eighth, ninth sense of something, that you know in some sort of capacity that something bad is going to happen, something is now developing-- I can tell you without any hesitation that a child feels that as well as an adult. The adult may look-- may already know it in more-- in a more experienced because of being able to identify quicker. But I already started feeling that this was, like, a terrible place we're going. And-- the-- because we now were seeing the abusiveness of the whole thing. People were still treated a little more humane, because they didn't want to see that-- that last minute of possible-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I wouldn't call it a revolution, but certainly a-- some kind of a resistance. So they, to that point, they were still trying to whitewash it. But on the way to the wagons, it was just like-- like an inferno. It was just like people just-- w were herded. And now the-- the-- the-- the beatings were going on. The schnell, schnell, schnell, fast, fast, schnell, schnell. And you can now see they were throwing people that couldn't walk fast enough. That's prior to the camps now. I'm talking prior to the Auschwitz, where we were going. There was no tolerance. There was nothing. There was zero. And now, my dad kept just saying, keep moving, keep moving, keep moving. And they kept saying, just don't look back. Just keep moving. They didn't want me to get hit and my sister was starting to cry and there was crying and screaming and crying. You know, hysteria was starting to become very, very clear.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was seven years-- I was-- No, I'm sorry. I was 11 years old. I was 10 and 1/2 at that point. 10 and 1/2. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was starting to be like a-- like a-- hys-- hysteria was everywhere. It was everywhere, OK? We got to the train station, and it was like instantaneously. There was no waiting. We went up on a ramp like a-- it was like a wooden plank with some blocks underneath. You know, because these cattle wagons were high, and the ground was obviously low. And there were no ladders or anything like that, so they put these wooden planks and they started herding us into these wagons. And all you kept hearing is schnell, fast, schnell, schnell. [INAUDIBLE]. You there, schnell, this, this, this, and this. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I can tell you that-- that the things that we did have to take, we couldn't get in, because we dropped it. And the only thing you wound up, maybe, was with some pillow or some pair of shoes or something and the-- and the clothes you wore. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they loaded us up. If this cattle car held 80 people, there must have been 150 people because I could not breathe. My dad had to restrain people, you know, so we could breathe. And the screaming and hollering and the-- that's Dante's inferno began. The beginning of the end just began right there for me. The nightmare was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you besides your parents and sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and-- well, our neighbors. Some of the neighbors, some people. You know, the ghetto was basically a small place and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't know everybody, but it's something that-- but that all goes out the window. Friends? It just goes out the window. The ba-- the-- the-- this-- the beginning of survival becomes the focus point. And unfortunately, I have to tell you that it takes precedence over everything else, the survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop now to change the tape.
